title: India's Innovation Paradox: Why the World's Third-Largest Startup Ecosystem Files Fewer Patents Than Denmark
subtitle: The data, the causes, and what practitioners can do about it
author: Shubham Sanjay Banne
excerpt: India mints startups at a remarkable rate - over 100,000 DPIIT-recognized startups as of 2024. Yet Indian companies file patents at a fraction of the rate of Chinese, South Korean, or German peers. This is not a funding problem, a talent problem, or an ambition problem. It is an IP culture problem - and it has a structural cause.

title: Ten Examiner Traps That Kill Good Patent Applications
subtitle: The most common drafting mistakes that give examiners ammunition
author: Shubham Sanjay Banne
excerpt: After years of drafting and prosecuting patents across India and the US, certain patterns emerge in applications that repeatedly attract difficult rejections. These are not obscure errors - they are common, teachable, and avoidable. Here are the ten drafting traps that consistently give examiners the leverage to reject otherwise valid inventions.

title: An Honest Assessment of AI for Patent Drafting in 2026
subtitle: What works, what doesn't, and what will get you into trouble
author: Shubham Sanjay Banne
excerpt: Every IP practitioner is now navigating a landscape where AI tools promise to transform patent drafting. The promise is partly real. But the risks - hallucination, scope errors, prosecution history problems, and professional responsibility traps - are just as real. This is a practitioner's honest assessment of where AI actually helps in 2026.

title: Section 3(k) and Software Patents in India: The State of Play in 2026
subtitle: What Indian patent practice currently accepts and rejects for software inventions
author: Shubham Sanjay Banne
excerpt: Section 3(k) of the Indian Patents Act excludes 'a mathematical method or a business method or a computer programme per se or algorithms.' Yet the Indian Patent Office grants software-related patents regularly. Understanding the line between 'per se' software and patentable technical innovation is the practitioner's essential skill.

title: Building a Patent Portfolio as an Indian Startup: A Practical Framework
subtitle: From first filing to strategic portfolio - a founder's guide
author: Shubham Sanjay Banne
excerpt: Most Indian startups approach patents reactively - filing defensively when the VC asks, or after a competitor scare. A strategic approach looks different: a small, carefully chosen filing program that protects the core innovation, signals seriousness to investors, and builds a moat with each new product iteration.
